name: Prairie Falcon
ecosystem: Canyon, arid cliff, Columbia Plateau basalt. Eastern PNW primary.
season: Resident (eastern PNW year-round; some altitudinal migration to valley floors in winter)
conservation: Least Concern (IUCN). Stable. 5,000-10,000 pairs US. Ground squirrel poisoning primary threat.

vocalization.description: Sharp staccato kik-ki-ki-ki. Shorter note attack than Peregrine kak. 5-15+ notes per burst.
vocalization.character: Falconidae family signature: rapid staccato. Slightly lower and shorter-noted than Peregrine.
vocalization.function: Nest defense (aggressive), territorial alarm, stoop accompaniment

ecology.nesting: Natural cliff ledges only (not urban buildings). Basalt canyon walls, granite outcrops, sandstone escarpments.
ecology.hunting: Low fast pursuit over open terrain (primary). High-altitude stoop (secondary). More horizontal than Peregrine.
ecology.prey: Ground squirrels (primary breeding season), horned larks, meadowlarks, starlings, other open-country birds
ecology.field_mark: Dark axillary patch (underwing base) -- diagnostic field mark distinguishing from Peregrine in flight

taxonomy_note.endemic: North American endemic. Does not breed in Europe, Asia, South America.
taxonomy_note.mexicanus_etymology: Type specimen from Mexico; primary range is western US, not Mexico
taxonomy_note.falco_genus: Second Falco in engine (231 Peregrine). Ecological contrast: cosmopolitan/urban vs. endemic/canyon.

connections.description: Gruntruck (E=7, hard rock, Roadrunner, Capitol Hill). E=13. Canyon specialist meets underground metal lineage.
Peregrine Falcon (FIRST FALCONIDAE, E=7). Two Falcos: cosmopolitan/urban vs. endemic/canyon.
Ferruginous Hawk (eastern PNW shrub-steppe). Both eastern PNW specialists, open-country hunters.
Bald Eagle (FIRST ACCIPITRIDAE). Raptor arc opened 221. Prairie Falcon at 232 closes the initial 12-degree run.
